text: The anaerobic threshold is the single variable with the highest predictive power for marathon performance, exceeding VO2max alone.
Marathon performance can be almost entirely explained by three variables together: VO2max, running economy (VO2 at 15 km/h), and fractional utilisation of VO2max at marathon race pace.
Marathon runners have a higher fat oxidation turnover rate than normal individuals at given high exercise intensities, both in absolute and relative terms.
High-volume endurance training raises the fractional utilisation of VO2max at marathon race pace by promoting metabolic adaptations that favor fat oxidation and delay fatigue.
There is probably an upper limit in training volume (kilometrage) above which no further improvements occur in the fractional utilisation of VO2max at marathon race pace.
VO2max and, to some extent, running economy appear to be limited by genetic factors in their response to training.

Large variations in VO2max exist among marathon runners with similar performance capacity, indicating that running economy and fractional VO2max utilisation are complementary determinants of performance.
